old_claim.note: Advanced Metallurgy (Medieval) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Transformer cores and conductors require metalworking/material supply, but medieval metallurgy is contextual ancestry rather than a direct transformer prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica transformer article describing primary/secondary coils and iron transformer cores.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports transformer component needs around windings and cores, not a broad medieval-metallurgy prerequisite.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This justifies keeping metalworking as contextual material ancestry while avoiding a misleading causal claim that medieval advanced metallurgy directly enables transformer invention.

ontology_before: The old edge made a broad medieval metallurgy node look like a direct transformer-enabling technology.
ontology_after: The edge is now a common dependency for material ancestry; copper windings and electromagnetism carry the stronger transformer dependencies.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant added in 2026-06-07-energy-edge-source-scope.json to lock the corrected Energy Systems edge semantics.
would_reject_if: A more specific primary or textbook source showed the original dependency type was globally required for the scoped technology.
The dependent node were narrowed to a later industrial product where the old prerequisite is truly mandatory.
A future source-check splits the node and moves this edge onto a more precise downstream technology.
short_reason: Transformer source supports metal components, not a direct medieval-metallurgy dependency.
